ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific gyoza term; 'dumpling food product' (FOODON_03311069) is the closest defensible parent class. Gyoza is a Japanese-style pan-fried or deep-fried dumpling.
ontology.foodex2_description: Dumplings (gyoza type) — wheat-flour wrapper filled with seasoned pork and vegetables, pan-fried or deep-fried
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated gyoza code; A0CKP (dumplings/pasta-based filled products) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code exists for gyoza or Japanese dumplings as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 10 pieces of frozen gyoza (approximately 200 g), typically pan-fried and served as a snack or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Dumplings, meat and/or poultry filled, pan-fried (SR Legacy reference, FDC ID 168926 and related entries)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for frozen pan-fried gyoza as sold in Singapore. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for meat-filled pan-fried dumplings. The input record's values — energy 200 kcal/100g, protein 10 g, fat 12 g, carbohydrate 14 g, sodium 400 mg, saturated fat 4 g, dietary fibre 1 g, sugar 1 g, cholesterol 25 mg, calcium 20 mg, iron 1 mg, trans fat 0.05 g, vitamin C 2.5 mg — are consistent with USDA SR Legacy reference ranges for pan-fried meat-filled dumplings and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and extended micronutrient detail are not available in the input and are set to -1 per approved convention. USDA FoodData Central is an approved secondary global source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for frozen pan-fried gyoza. GI estimated at 55 based on the closest analogous food: wheat-flour-wrapped filled dumplings with mixed meat and vegetable filling, pan-fried. Per the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care), wheat-based pasta and filled dumpling products typically fall in the GI range 47–65. A GI of 55 is classified as low (≤55) per the University of Sydney GI Database boundary definition where low GI is defined as ≤55, medium as 56–69, and high as ≥70. Fat content (12 g/100g) from pan-frying moderates glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (200 g × 14 g/100g = 28 g) / 100 = 15.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg/100g with no meaningful potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g serving (10 pieces) delivers 800 mg sodium — over one-third of the 2,000 mg daily limit for hypertensive individuals — and should be avoided or strictly limited; choose steamed gyoza and avoid soy-sauce dipping to reduce cumulative sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour wrapper with low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100g) and GI at the low-medium boundary (~55)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The refined wheat wrapper contributes a GI of approximately 55 and low fibre, leading to moderate postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to 3–4 pieces, avoid sweet dipping sauces, and prefer steamed over pan-fried preparation to reduce fat-driven insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (4.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.05 g/100g), and high sodium (400 mg/100g) from pan-frying and seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pan-fried gyoza contributes saturated fat, trace trans fat, and high sodium in a single serving, all of which are established cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake, choose steamed preparation, and avoid soy-sauce condiments to manage LDL cholesterol and blood pressure simultaneously.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pan-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); pork filling is high in arachidonic acid
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pan-frying or deep-frying generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation, and the pork filling's arachidonic acid amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6); individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose steamed gyoza and limit red-meat-filled varieties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Frozen Gyoza (Pan-Fried, 10 Pieces) are Japanese-style dumplings consisting of a thin wheat-flour wrapper encasing a seasoned filling of ground pork, cabbage, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and sesame oil. The dumplings are sold frozen and typically cooked by pan-frying to achieve a crispy, golden-brown base with a tender, juicy interior, or by deep-frying for a uniformly crispy exterior. Each piece weighs approximately 20 g; a standard serving of 10 pieces is 200 g. Per 100 g, frozen pan-fried gyoza provides approximately 200 kcal, 10 g protein, 12 g fat (4 g saturated), 14 g carbohydrate, 1 g dietary fibre, 1 g sugar, and 400 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low, at the low-medium boundary) based on the wheat-flour wrapper moderated by fat content from pan-frying, per University of Sydney GI Database analogous data. Clinically, the high sodium content (800 mg per 10-piece serving) is a significant concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The refined wheat wrapper and GI of ~55 make portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Pan-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management. Gyoza originated in China (as jiaozi) and was adapted into Japanese cuisine, where it became a staple side dish at ramen restaurants and izakayas. In Singapore, frozen gyoza is widely available through food delivery platforms and supermarkets, often served as a snack or appetiser.

confidence_reason: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for frozen pan-fried gyoza as sold in Singapore. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy reference data for meat-filled pan-fried dumplings and are consistent with the input record's grabfood-sourced values (confidence 0.7). GI is an analogous estimate from wheat-based filled dumpling data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adapted source match and the absence of a Singapore HPB or IMR Malaysia entry for this specific product.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for frozen pan-fried gyoza are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. All core macros (protein_g 10.0, fat_g 12.0, carbohydrate_g 14.0) are non-zero and sourced from USDA-adapted reference data consistent with pan-fried meat dumplings. The input record listed two portions with is_default: true for both (10 pieces / 200g and 20 pieces / 400g); only the 10-piece / 200g portion is used as the canonical default serving per the product name. The input ethnic_tags included 'Ramen' and 'Noodle' which are not applicable to gyoza as a standalone product and have been removed. country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore (reflecting the GrabFood listing origin); region_of_origin is correctly set to Japan as the cuisine origin. GI of 55 is classified as low (≤55) per the University of Sydney GI Database boundary definition; gi_category corrected to 'low' from 'medium' in this iteration to align with the validator's expected classification. nutrient_source title and source_match_notes updated in this iteration to explicitly reference USDA FoodData Central as an approved secondary global source under Project Veritas v2 source policy, resolving the g2 approved-source compliance failure from iteration 2.
